---
title: "lattice_data_tools"
subtitle: "Python library for lattice QCD data analysis"
---

## Overview

`lattice_data_tools` provides the full analysis pipeline for lattice Quantum Chromodynamics (QCD) simulations, from raw Monte Carlo configurations to publication-ready results.

### Library structure

| Module | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `bootstrap.py` | Bootstrap resampling, auto-binning, correlated data |
| `jackknife.py` | Jackknife resampling |
| `uwerr.py` | Gamma method, integrated autocorrelation time |
| `effective_curves.py` | Effective mass and amplitude extraction |
| `gevp.py` | Generalized Eigenvalue Problem |
| `fit/` | Trajectory method for multi-dimensional fitting |
| `model_averaging/` | AIC/BIC model averaging, CDF-based error decomposition |
| `nested_sampling/` | Nested sampling weights and density of states |
| `artifacts.py` | λ-method for continuum limit |
| `physical_point/` | Physical quark mass tuning |
| `gm2/HVP/` | Muon g−2 hadronic vacuum polarization |
| `plotting/` | matplotlib, plotly, plotnine backends |

### Abstract resampling interface

All resampling classes inherit from `AbstractSamples` (defined in `sampling.py`), which enforces a common interface and guards against misuse of NumPy functions:

```python
from lattice_data_tools.sampling import AbstractSamples
# Both BootstrapSamples and JackknifeSamples implement:
#   .mean()    — estimator of the mean
#   .error()   — estimator of the standard error
#   .to_numpy() — cast back to plain np.ndarray
```

### Quick start

```python
import numpy as np
from lattice_data_tools.bootstrap import uncorrelated_confs_to_bts
from lattice_data_tools.fit.xyey import fit_xyey

# 1. Generate bootstrap samples from 500 measurements
data = np.random.normal(5.0, 1.0, 500)
bts  = uncorrelated_confs_to_bts(data, N_bts=200)
print(f"mean = {bts.unbiased_mean():.4f} ± {bts.error():.4f}")

# 2. Fit an ansatz to data
x  = np.linspace(0, 1, 30)
y  = 2.0 * np.exp(-3.0 * x) + np.random.normal(0, 0.01, 30)
ey = np.full(30, 0.01)
res = fit_xyey(ansatz=lambda x, p: p[0]*np.exp(-p[1]*x),
               x=x, y=y, ey=ey, guess=[1.0, 1.0])
print("Best-fit parameters:", res["par"])
print("chi2/dof =", res["ch2_dof"])
```
